title: Geometry: Scale, Angles & Circles
blurb: Scale drawings, angle relationships, the triangle inequality, and circles — where π finally earns its name.

lessons.title: Scale Drawings
lessons.concreteComparison: An architect's blueprint says “scale $1:50$” — every $1$ cm on paper is $50$ cm of real wall. Your bedroom, $4$ m wide, becomes an $8$ cm rectangle you can slide around the page. Scale drawings shrink the world onto paper (or blow bacteria up onto posters) while keeping every proportion intact — they're proportional relationships you can SEE.
lessons.objective: Use scale factors to convert between drawing lengths and real lengths, and rescale areas correctly.
concept: A **scale** like $1:50$ (or “$1$ cm $= 50$ cm”) is a ratio between drawing length and true length. It's a constant of proportionality wearing a hard hat.
Drawing → real: MULTIPLY by the scale factor. Real → drawing: DIVIDE. An $8$ cm blueprint wall at $1:50$ is $8 \times 50 = 400$ cm $= 4$ m.
Different drawings of the same object have different scales; to compare or redraw, go THROUGH the real size.
Areas scale by the SQUARE of the factor: at $1:50$, $1$ cm² of paper is $50 \times 50 = 2500$ cm² of floor. Doubling a photo's width and height quadruples its area.
lessons.keyIdea: Scale is a ratio: multiply to go big, divide to go small — and square the factor for areas.

facts.title: Lengths scale linearly
facts.explanation: One multiplication converts drawing to reality.

facts.title: Areas scale by the square
facts.explanation: Both directions stretch, so area picks up the factor twice.

workedExamples.statement: A blueprint uses the scale $1:50$. A wall is $8$ cm on paper. How long is the real wall in meters?
A map's scale is $2$ cm $: 15$ km. Two cities are $9$ cm apart on the map. Real distance?
A model car is built at $1:24$. The real car is $4.8$ m long. How long is the model in cm?

commonMistakes.correction: Real → drawing DIVIDES by the scale factor. A 480 cm car at 1:24 becomes 20 cm — if your model came out bigger than the car, flip the operation.

commonMistakes.text: Scaling area by the factor once.
commonMistakes.correction: At scale 3, areas grow by $3^2 = 9$, not 3. A 2×3 photo tripled is 6×9: area 54, nine times 6.

tryIt.statement: At $1:200$, a park path is $12$ cm on the plan. Real length in meters?
A drawing is enlarged by a factor of $4$. What happens to its area?

steps.title: One shape, every size
steps.teach: Drag the scale factor and watch the copy grow and shrink. Sides multiply by the factor — and keep an eye on the area readout: it moves FASTER.

steps.title: The ratio with a hard hat
beats: $1:50$ means $1$ unit on paper $= 50$ in the world.
Drawing → real: MULTIPLY by $50$.
Real → drawing: DIVIDE by $50$.
It's a constant of proportionality — Topic 1, on a blueprint.

steps.title: Areas pick it up twice
beats: Scale a rectangle by $k$: width $\times k$ AND height $\times k$.
So area $\times k^2$.
$1:50$ → paper cm² is $2500$ real cm².
Double a pizza's diameter and you get FOUR times the pizza.

steps.title: Convert it
steps.prompt: A map's scale is $1:25{,}000$. A trail is $6$ cm on the map. Real length?

steps.title: Both directions, plus area
steps.intro: Multiply out, divide in, square for area.

examples.prompt: A poster of a $0.5$ mm ant is drawn at $200:1$. How big is the ant on the poster?
A room is $6$ m by $4$ m. At $1:100$, what are the drawing's dimensions and area?
steps: Area: $24$ cm² (real: $24$ m² — the factor squared connects them: $24 \text{ m}^2 = 240000 \text{ cm}^2 = 24 \times 100^2$).

examples.prompt: Two maps show the same $30$ km road: Map A at $1$ cm $: 5$ km, Map B at $1$ cm $: 3$ km. How long is the road on each?

steps.title: Scale fluency
steps.intro: Which direction? Then multiply or divide.

problems.prompt: At $1:20$, a table is drawn $9$ cm long. Real length?
A $150$ m bridge must fit a $30$ cm page. Which scale works exactly?
A photo is scaled up by factor $5$. Its area becomes…

steps.title: The map that ate Manhattan
steps.body: Jorge Luis Borges wrote a one-paragraph story about an empire whose cartographers made a map at scale $1:1$ — exactly as big as the empire itself, and therefore perfectly useless. Every real map is a compromise: the scale factor decides what fits in your pocket and what detail dies. GPS apps re-pick the scale every time you pinch the screen.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A scale is a drawing:real ratio — multiply to real, divide to drawing.
Route comparisons through the real size.
Areas scale by the factor SQUARED.

lessons.title: Angle Relationships
lessons.concreteComparison: Scissors open: as the blades spread, the angle between them grows — and the angle on the OTHER side of the pivot grows identically. Cross two sticks and you've made two pairs of equal angles and four pairs that sum to $180°$. Angles come in relationships, and the relationships let you find every angle from just one.
lessons.objective: Use complementary, supplementary, vertical, and adjacent angle relationships to find unknown angles — with equations.
concept: **Complementary** angles sum to $90°$ (they complete a corner); **supplementary** angles sum to $180°$ (they complete a straight line).
**Vertical** angles — the X-shape's opposite pair — are always EQUAL. **Adjacent** angles share a ray and add along whatever they fill.
One known angle unlocks the rest: if two lines cross and one angle is $35°$, its vertical twin is $35°$ and its two neighbors are $145°$ each.
Grade 7's upgrade: the unknown can be an expression. If $(2x + 10)°$ and $(3x - 20)°$ are supplementary, then $2x + 10 + 3x - 20 = 180$ — an equation from a picture.
lessons.keyIdea: Complements make 90°, supplements make 180°, verticals are equal — and each relationship is an equation waiting to be solved.

facts.title: Angles as equations
facts.explanation: Angle relationships turn diagrams into solvable equations.

workedExamples.statement: Two angles are complementary; one measures $34°$. Find the other.
Two lines cross, making an angle of $35°$. Find the other three angles.
workedExamples.solution: Vertical twin: $35°$. The two neighbors: $180 - 35 = 145°$ each.

workedExamples.statement: Angles $(2x + 10)°$ and $(3x - 20)°$ are supplementary. Find $x$ and both angles.

commonMistakes.correction: Complementary = Corner = 90°; Supplementary = Straight = 180°. The C's and S's line up — use them.
EQUAL is the vertical pair (across the X). Adjacent angles along a line SUM to 180° — they're only equal if both are 90°.

tryIt.statement: An angle measures $67°$. Find its complement and its supplement.

steps.title: The X-machine
steps.teach: Tilt the crossing line. Watch the vertical pair stay locked in step while each adjacent pair keeps summing to 180° — the relationships hold at every tilt.

steps.title: Four relationships
beats: **Complementary**: two angles summing to $90°$.
**Supplementary**: two angles summing to $180°$.
**Vertical**: opposite angles at an X — always equal.
**Adjacent**: sharing a ray — they add along what they fill.

steps.title: Corner split
steps.teach: A right angle split by a ray: the two pieces always total 90°. Slide the ray — as one grows the other shrinks, complement style.

steps.title: One unlocks all
steps.prompt: Two lines cross; one angle is $72°$. What are the OTHER three?

steps.title: Angles by algebra
steps.intro: Pick the relationship, write the equation, solve.

examples.prompt: Two complementary angles are $(3x)°$ and $(2x + 15)°$. Find both.
An angle is $24°$ more than its supplement's half. The two are supplementary. Find the angle. (Let the supplement be $s$.)
Three angles fill a straight line: $x°$, $(2x)°$, and $60°$. Find $x$.

steps.title: Relationship radar
steps.intro: Name the relationship first; the equation follows.

problems.prompt: Angles of $(5x)°$ and $(4x)°$ make a straight line. $x = $ ?
An angle equals its own complement. It measures…
Vertical angles measure $(6x - 10)°$ and $(4x + 30)°$. Each angle is…

steps.title: Eratosthenes strikes again
steps.body: The same angle relationships you just used measured the planet: sunlight hitting Alexandria at $7.2°$ while striking Syene head-on creates equal alternate angles at Earth's center — so the two cities span $7.2°$ of the planet's $360°$. Vertical and supplementary pairs are the grammar; the Earth's circumference was the first great sentence.

steps.title: What you learned
points: One known angle at a crossing unlocks all four.
Angle relationships are equations — solve them like Topic 3.

lessons.title: Triangles: Possible or Not?
lessons.concreteComparison: Take sticks of lengths $3$, $4$, and $10$ and try to make a triangle. The $3$ and $4$ reach toward each other over the $10$… and never touch: $3 + 4 = 7 < 10$. Some triangles simply cannot exist. Two quick tests — one for sides, one for angles — decide which blueprints are buildable.
lessons.objective: Apply the triangle inequality and the 180° angle sum to decide whether triangles can exist and to find missing angles.
concept: **Triangle inequality:** the two shorter sides must together EXCEED the longest side. $3, 4, 10$ fails ($7 \le 10$); $3, 4, 6$ works ($7 > 6$).
Exactly equal is still a fail: $3 + 4 = 7$ with a third side of $7$ collapses flat — a segment, not a triangle.
**Angle sum:** a triangle's three angles always total $180°$. Two angles determine the third: $180 - 65 - 48 = 67°$.
The two tests are independent: sides decide if the shape can close; angles describe how it bends once it does. Grade 7 uses both as existence checks and equation generators.
lessons.keyIdea: Sides: shortest two must out-reach the longest. Angles: all three sum to exactly 180°.

facts.title: Triangle inequality
facts.explanation: The two shorter sides must strictly beat the longest.

facts.title: Angle sum
facts.explanation: Every triangle, every shape, every size — always 180.

workedExamples.statement: Can side lengths $3, 4, 10$ form a triangle?
workedExamples.solution: Check the longest: $3 + 4 = 7 \le 10$ — NO, they can't reach.

workedExamples.statement: Can side lengths $5, 7, 11$ form a triangle?
A triangle has angles $65°$ and $48°$. Find the third.

commonMistakes.text: Checking only one pair of sides.
commonMistakes.correction: The test that matters is shortest-two vs longest. (If they pass, the other pairs pass automatically.) For 3, 4, 10 test 3 + 4 against 10 — not 4 + 10 against 3.

commonMistakes.text: Calling a + b = c a valid triangle.
commonMistakes.correction: Equality collapses the triangle flat. The inequality is STRICT: $a + b > c$, not $\ge$.

steps.title: Can it close?
steps.teach: Adjust the three side lengths and watch the arms try to meet. The moment the two shorter sides stop out-reaching the longest, the triangle dies flat.

steps.title: The reach test
beats: Lay the longest side down as a base.
The other two must REACH over it and touch.
$a + b > c$ (with $c$ the longest).
$3 + 4 > 6$? Yes → triangle. $\;3 + 4 > 10$? No → flat-pack.

steps.title: The 180° budget
beats: Every triangle spends exactly $180°$ across its three angles.
Two known angles fix the third: $180 - 65 - 48 = 67°$.
Algebra version: $x + 2x + 3x = 180$.
No triangle ever goes over budget — or under.

steps.title: Buildable?
steps.prompt: Which set of sides CAN form a triangle?

steps.title: Existence and equations
steps.intro: Reach test for sides; budget test for angles.

examples.prompt: Two sides of a triangle are $5$ and $9$. What lengths are possible for the third side $x$?
A triangle's angles are $(2x)°$, $(3x)°$, and $(x + 30)°$. Find all three.
An isosceles triangle has a $40°$ angle between its two equal sides. Find the base angles.
steps: Base angles are equal: call each $b$.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has a $23°$ angle. The third angle is…
Two sides are $6$ and $11$. Which third side is IMPOSSIBLE?

steps.title: Triangles hold up the world
steps.body: Bridges, cranes, and cell towers are lattices of triangles for one reason: a triangle is the only polygon that CANNOT flex without changing a side length. A square can shear into a rhombus; a triangle with fixed sides is rigid — the triangle inequality's strict “greater than” is literally what keeps the structure standing.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Triangle inequality: shortest two sides must STRICTLY out-reach the longest.
Third-side range: between the difference and the sum of the other two.
Angles always total 180° — an equation generator for missing angles.

lessons.title: Circles & Circumference
lessons.concreteComparison: Wrap a tape measure around a bike wheel: about $220$ cm. Measure straight across: about $70$ cm. Divide: $220 \div 70 \approx 3.14$. Do it with a coin, a pizza, a hula hoop — the same $3.14...$ appears every time. That stubborn ratio is $\pi$, and “circumference $= \pi d$” is just this experiment written as a formula.
lessons.objective: Use C = πd = 2πr to find circumference, diameter, or radius.
concept: In every circle, the **circumference** (distance around) divided by the **diameter** (distance across) equals $\pi \approx 3.14159...$ — the same for wheels and planets.
So $C = \pi d$, and since the diameter is two radii, $C = 2\pi r$.
The formula runs backwards: a circle with $C = 31.4$ cm has $d = \frac{31.4}{\pi} \approx 10$ cm.
Answers can stay EXACT in terms of $\pi$ ($C = 14\pi$) or go decimal with an approximation ($\approx 43.98$). Exact-in-π is the mathematician's habit; decimals are the engineer's.
lessons.keyIdea: $C = \pi d = 2\pi r$ — around is π times across, in every circle ever drawn.

facts.title: The definition of π
facts.explanation: Pi IS the around-to-across ratio — the formula just rearranges it.

workedExamples.statement: A wheel has diameter $70$ cm. Find its circumference (use $\pi \approx \frac{22}{7}$).
A circular garden has radius $4.5$ m. Find its circumference in terms of $\pi$.
A trundle wheel clicks once per meter of circumference. What diameter does it need? (Use $\pi \approx 3.14$.)

commonMistakes.text: Using the radius in C = πd.
commonMistakes.correction: $C = \pi d$ wants the DIAMETER. With radius 5, either double first ($d = 10$) or use $C = 2\pi r$ — don't feed the 5 to πd.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying by π when solving backwards.
commonMistakes.correction: From circumference to diameter you DIVIDE: $d = C \div \pi$. If your recovered diameter is bigger than the circumference, you multiplied.

tryIt.statement: Find the circumference of a circle with radius $7$ (use $\pi \approx \frac{22}{7}$).
A circle has circumference $18\pi$. What is its radius?

steps.title: Unroll the circle
steps.teach: Watch the circle's rim peel off and lie flat: it stretches just past three diameters — 3.14159... of them. That's π, live and in person.

steps.title: π is a ratio, not a decoration
beats: Around $\div$ across $= 3.14159...$ for EVERY circle.
We named the ratio $\pi$.
$C = \pi d$ — that's the experiment as a formula.
Radius version: $C = 2\pi r$.

steps.title: Exact vs approximate
beats: Exact: $C = 14\pi$ cm. Clean, precise, algebra-friendly.
Approximate: $C \approx 43.98$ cm, using $\pi \approx 3.14$.
$\frac{22}{7}$ is a handy fraction stand-in when 7 divides things.
Say WHICH you're using — answers differ slightly.

steps.title: Around from across
steps.prompt: A circular table has diameter $1.4$ m. Its circumference is about… (use $\pi \approx \frac{22}{7}$)

steps.title: Forwards and backwards
steps.intro: πd forward, C ÷ π backward, 2πr when you're given the radius.

examples.prompt: A jar lid has radius $3.5$ cm. Circumference? (π ≈ 22/7)
A running track's inner circle measures $400$ m around. Its diameter? (π ≈ 3.14)
A wheel of diameter $60$ cm rolls forward $10$ full turns. How far does it travel? (π ≈ 3.14)

steps.title: π practice
steps.intro: Pick the right form of the formula first.

problems.prompt: Radius $10$ cm. Circumference in terms of π?
A pizza cutter wheel (diameter $7$ cm) rolls one full turn. Distance? (π ≈ 22/7)

steps.title: π has been dug to 100 trillion digits
steps.body: Engineers need only 40 digits of π to compute the circumference of the observable universe to within one atom — yet computers have calculated over 100 trillion digits, mostly to stress-test hardware. The digits never repeat and never end: π is irrational, the first one most people ever meet, hiding inside every wheel they've ever ridden.

steps.title: What you learned
points: π is the around-to-across ratio of every circle.
$C = \pi d = 2\pi r$; divide by π to go backwards.
Exact-in-π or decimal-approximate — know which you're giving.

lessons.title: Area of Circles
lessons.concreteComparison: Slice a pizza into $16$ thin wedges and lay them tip-to-tail, alternating up and down. They form a bumpy near-rectangle: height $=$ the radius, width $=$ half the crust ($\pi r$). Its area: $\pi r \times r = \pi r^2$. The circle-area formula isn't magic — it's pizza, rearranged.
lessons.objective: Use A = πr² to find a circle's area, work backwards to the radius, and handle half- and quarter-circles.
concept: A circle's **area** is $A = \pi r^2$ — the wedge-rearrangement makes a near-rectangle of width $\pi r$ and height $r$.
The radius gets SQUARED before π: radius $6$ gives $A = 36\pi$, not $12\pi$. Given a diameter, halve it first.
Backwards: from $A = 49\pi$, the radius satisfies $r^2 = 49$, so $r = 7$.
Fractions of circles scale the area: a semicircle is $\frac{1}{2}\pi r^2$; a quarter circle $\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2$. Composite shapes add or subtract circle pieces from rectangles.
lessons.keyIdea: $A = \pi r^2$ — square the radius, then multiply by π; halves and quarters scale accordingly.

facts.title: The formula
facts.explanation: Width πr times height r — the pizza rectangle.

workedExamples.statement: Find the area of a circle with radius $6$ cm, in terms of $\pi$.
A circular rug has diameter $14$ dm. Find its area (use $\pi \approx \frac{22}{7}$).
A circle has area $49\pi$ m². Find its radius and circumference.

commonMistakes.correction: $A = \pi r^2$ uses the RADIUS. Diameter 14 → radius 7 → $49\pi$, not $196\pi$.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying r by 2 instead of squaring.
commonMistakes.correction: $r^2$ means $r \times r$: radius 6 gives $36\pi$, not $12\pi$. Squaring wins over doubling as soon as $r > 2$.

tryIt.statement: Area of a circle with radius $9$, in terms of π?
A semicircular window has radius $2$ m. Its area? (exact)

steps.title: Wedges into rectangles
steps.teach: A sector is a pizza wedge of the circle. Watch how the wedge's area scales with its angle — the full 360° pie is πr², and every slice takes its fair share.

steps.title: Pizza proof
beats: Slice the circle into thin wedges.
Alternate them tip-up, tip-down: a bumpy rectangle.
Width $= \pi r$ (half the crust), height $= r$.
Area $= \pi r \times r = \pi r^2$.

steps.title: Square the RADIUS
beats: Given a diameter? Halve it first.
Backwards from area: un-square. $A = 49\pi \to r = 7$.
Half circle: $\frac{1}{2}\pi r^2$. Quarter: $\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2$.

steps.title: Square first
steps.prompt: A circle has radius $5$. Its area in terms of π is…

examples.prompt: A sprinkler waters a circle of radius $12$ m. What area does it cover? (π ≈ 3.14)
A quarter-circle garden bed has radius $6$ m. Its area, exactly?
A ring (annulus) has outer radius $10$ and inner radius $6$. Area of the ring, exactly?

steps.title: πr² workout
steps.intro: Radius in, square it, π it.

problems.prompt: Diameter $16$. Area in terms of π?
problems.explanation: $r = 8 \to A = 64\pi$. Squaring the diameter gives the wrong $256\pi$.

problems.prompt: A circle's area is $121\pi$. Its radius is…
A semicircular stage has radius $4$ m. Its floor area is… (exact)

steps.title: Why pizza size is sneaky
steps.body: A 12-inch pizza has area $36\pi \approx 113$ in²; a 16-inch has $64\pi \approx 201$ in² — nearly DOUBLE, from just 4 more inches of diameter. That's $r^2$ at work: area grows with the square. Pizza economists (a real hobby) have shown one large is almost always more pizza per dollar than two mediums. Geometry pays for dinner.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Halve diameters before squaring; un-square to go backwards.
Half/quarter circles and rings: scale or subtract full-circle areas.

lessons.title: Composite Figures & Solids
lessons.concreteComparison: A running track is a rectangle with two semicircular caps. A pool deck is a big rectangle minus the pool. An L-shaped room is two rectangles taped together. Real shapes are COMPOSITES — built by adding and subtracting the simple pieces you already own — and real objects have volume and surface area built the same way.
lessons.objective: Find areas of composite 2D figures and the volume and surface area of right prisms.
concept: **Decompose:** slice a composite shape into rectangles, triangles, and circle parts. Add the pieces — or subtract holes from a surrounding shape.
A track with a $40 \times 20$ rectangle and two semicircular ends of radius $10$: rectangle $800$ plus one full circle $100\pi$ — the two semicircles reunite.
**Volume of a prism** $=$ area of the base $\times$ height: $V = Bh$. A box, a tent, a Toblerone — same rule, different base.
**Surface area** $=$ the sum of every face — think of unfolding the box into its net and adding the rectangles. For a prism: two bases plus the side walls (base perimeter × height).
lessons.keyIdea: Slice 2D composites into knowns; for prisms, V = (base area) × height and SA = sum of the faces.

facts.title: Composite strategy
facts.explanation: Every weird shape is simple shapes in a trench coat.

facts.title: Prism volume
facts.explanation: Base area times height — whatever shape the base is.

workedExamples.statement: An L-shaped room is a $6 \times 4$ m rectangle with a $3 \times 2$ m rectangle attached. Total area?
A track is a $40 \times 20$ m rectangle with semicircular caps of radius $10$ m on both ends. Total area? (exact)
workedExamples.solution: Rectangle: $800$. Two semicircles $=$ one circle: $\pi(10^2) = 100\pi$. Total: $800 + 100\pi$ m².

workedExamples.statement: A triangular prism has a base triangle of area $12$ cm² and length $9$ cm. Volume?

commonMistakes.text: Double-counting the seam of a decomposition.
commonMistakes.correction: When you slice an L-shape, the cut line is INTERNAL — count each region once and never add the seam's 'wall'.
Volume fills the inside (cubic units); surface area wraps the outside (square units). A box of 24 cm³ might need 52 cm² of wrapping paper.

tryIt.statement: A square of side $8$ has a circular hole of radius $2$. Remaining area? (exact)
A box measures $5 \times 4 \times 3$ cm. Volume and surface area?

steps.title: Shapes in a trench coat
steps.teach: Toggle the decomposition and watch a composite figure fall apart into rectangles you already know. Add the pieces; that's the whole trick.

steps.title: Slice, add, subtract
beats: See a weird shape? Find the simple shapes inside.
L-shape → two rectangles. Track → rectangle + circle.
Holes SUBTRACT: deck $=$ big rectangle $-$ pool.
Label each piece's dimensions before computing anything.

steps.title: The prism rule
steps.teach: A prism is a base stamped through space. Spin the box: its volume is the base area times the height, and its surface is the six faces you can count.

steps.title: Decompose it
steps.prompt: A figure is a $10 \times 6$ rectangle plus a right triangle (base $6$, height $4$) on one end. Total area?

examples.prompt: A window is a $3 \times 2$ m rectangle topped by a semicircle of radius $1$ m. Total area? (π ≈ 3.14)
A swimming pool deck: a $12 \times 8$ m rectangle of tiles minus the $8 \times 4$ m pool. Deck area?
A tent is a triangular prism: base triangle of base $4$ m and height $3$ m, length $5$ m. Volume?

steps.title: Build and unbuild
steps.intro: Pieces for area, base × height for volume.

problems.prompt: A $9 \times 9$ square loses a $3 \times 3$ corner. Remaining area?
A cereal box is $20 \times 6 \times 30$ cm. Its volume?
Surface area of a cube with edge $4$?

steps.title: Why cans are cylinders
steps.body: Drink makers care about surface area per volume: metal costs money, and the shape that wraps the most volume in the least material (among easy-to-make shapes) is the cylinder with height ≈ diameter. Real soda cans are slightly taller than optimal — because hands, shelves, and vending machines vote too. Every can on the shelf is a solved composite-surface-area problem.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Composites decompose: add the pieces, subtract the holes.
Prism volume $= Bh$; surface area $=$ sum of the faces (unfold the net).

practice.statement: At a scale of $1:75$, a hallway is $6$ cm on the plan. Real length in meters?
An angle's supplement is four times its complement. Find the angle.
Two lines cross; one angle is $(3x + 6)°$ and its vertical twin is $(5x - 30)°$. Find the angle.
A triangle's angles are $(x)°$, $(x + 15)°$, and $(2x - 35)°$. Find them all.
A circular fountain has circumference $44$ m. Its diameter? (π ≈ 22/7)
Find the area of a circle with diameter $18$ cm, in terms of π.
A gift box is $12 \times 8 \times 5$ cm. Find its volume and surface area.

testYourself.statement: A model plane at $1:48$ is $25$ cm long. How long is the real plane in meters?
Angles $(2x)°$ and $(x + 30)°$ are complementary. Find both angles.
Two sides of a triangle measure $9$ and $14$. Give the full range for the third side $x$.
A bike wheel has radius $35$ cm. How far does the bike travel in $100$ wheel turns? (π ≈ 22/7)
A circular pond of radius $3$ m sits in a $10 \times 8$ m lawn. Grass area? (exact)
A juice carton is a prism with a $7 \times 4$ cm base and height $15$ cm. Its volume in liters? ($1$ L $= 1000$ cm³)
